output:
# Resolving the SIRC Gap in DiffRate Token Compression with DART: Disentangled-Axis, Readout-marginalized Token Compression

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

Vision Transformers (ViTs) [1] deliver strong accuracy but are compute-heavy, and their self-attention and MLP cost scales with the number of tokens |V| (attention is ~O(|V|^2), MLP is ~O(|V|)). Because of this scaling, token compression -- dropping or fusing tokens in intermediate blocks -- has become the dominant FLOPs lever for deploying ViT and DeiT [2] backbones, as well as MAE-pretrained [3] models, without redesigning the architecture or retraining from scratch. Hierarchical designs such as Swin [20] reduce token counts structurally, but for standard isotropic ViTs the deployment path of choice for edge inference, real-time vision, and cost-sensitive serving is to compress tokens inside an otherwise fixed backbone.

The field splits into two historically separate sub-fields. Token PRUNING drops dispensable tokens and includes DynamicViT [6], EViT [7], A-ViT [8], Evo-ViT [9], SPViT [10], IA-RED2 [15], and ATS [16]; these methods use learned or attention-based importance to decide which tokens to delete. Token MERGING instead fuses redundant tokens: ToMe [4] uses pairwise key-similarity with a fixed per-block schedule, Token Pooling [18] clusters similar tokens, and the same merging idea transfers to generative models such as ToMe-for-diffusion [19]. Pruning asks "is this token dispensable?" while merging asks "is this token redundant with others?" -- two structurally different questions that evolved with distinct lineages and benchmarks.

DiffRate [5] is the state-of-the-art unification of these two lines: it combines prune and merge and makes the per-block compression RATE differentiable under a hard FLOPs budget, reporting roughly a 30% FLOPs reduction at near-zero Top-1 loss on ImageNet-1k. To rank tokens, DiffRate reads a SINGLE importance scalar per token from ONE class-token (CLS) attention map -- one layer, one head, a single clean forward pass. The unexamined, load-bearing assumption is that this one importance scalar, read from one attention map, can simultaneously decide which tokens to DROP and which to MERGE. ToFu / Token Fusion [17] explicitly bridges pruning and merging, but still derives both decisions from one importance/similarity pipeline. NAS-style differentiable-budget machinery such as ProxylessNAS [13] and ViT-Slim [14] supplies the tools to learn a discrete allocation under a hard cost constraint, but operates over channels and architecture, not over a per-block prune-vs-merge token partition.

A separate and more recent literature diagnoses a structural problem with attention-based importance. Darcet et al. [11] ("Vision Transformers Need Registers", ICLR 2024) show that ViTs spontaneously allocate large CLS-attention mass to a few high-norm "register"-like tokens for reasons UNRELATED to semantic content, and the attention-sink line (Xiao et al. [12]) shows the analogous effect in transformers more broadly. This literature prescribes ARCHITECTURAL fixes (add register tokens) or serving tricks (keep sink tokens in the KV cache), but it never feeds the artifact diagnosis back into a token-compression operator. As a result, a single-readout importance score mixes (a) genuine task dispensability and redundancy with (b) attention-sink/register salience and (c) position and scale effects -- and no existing compression method strips (b) and (c) out before ranking tokens.

The stakes of getting the keep-set right are large and quantifiable. DiffRate's own Fig-1 comparison shows that at a MATCHED compression budget, accuracy swings widely with the keep-set CHOICE rather than the rate: EViT [7] degrades toward 73.8 Top-1 where DiffRate [5] holds 78.8 and ToMe [4] sits at 78.0, against a ~79.8 uncompressed DeiT-S reference. A ~5-point Top-1 gap at identical FLOPs is attributable entirely to WHICH tokens are kept, merged, or pruned -- i.e., to the ranking signal, not the budget mechanism. If that signal is contaminated by artifacts, the loss is silent: it hides inside an otherwise-good trade-off curve.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

We name the gap SIRC -- Single-readout Importance-Redundancy Conflation. DiffRate's keep/prune/merge partition is driven by ONE scalar per token, an importance score read from ONE CLS-attention map, and that single signal is forced to do two structurally different jobs while being contaminated by causes that demand different actions.

ONE SIGNAL, TWO DECISIONS. Pruning asks whether a token is DISPENSABLE (low task value, safe to delete); merging asks whether a token is REDUNDANT WITH OTHERS (high value but duplicated, safe to fuse). These are different questions. A token can be highly important yet redundant (it should be merged), or unimportant yet unique (it should be pruned). DiffRate ranks both decisions off the same importance ordering, so the prune-share and the merge-share of a block are slaved to one axis and cannot independently express these two cases.

THREE CONFLATED CAUSES inside that single readout. The CLS-attention scalar mixes: (a) genuine task-relevant redundancy and dispensability -- the signal we actually want; (b) attention-artifact salience -- attention sinks and high-norm register-like tokens that absorb CLS attention for non-semantic reasons [11][12], inflating or deflating scores; and (c) position and scale effects -- center bias, token area, and per-block magnitude drift that shift the ordering independent of content. When (b) or (c) dominate, DiffRate can keep an attention-sink token while dropping a semantically unique one, or merge tokens that should have been pruned. The keep-set is then selected on the WRONG AXIS, costing accuracy at a fixed FLOPs budget.

This conflation also explains a known empirical weakness. DiffRate's imperfect cross-architecture rate TRANSFER (its Fig-6) follows naturally if the importance ordering is READOUT-RELATIVE: each backbone has a structure-specific artifact profile, so a single-axis rate schedule learned on one model cannot transfer cleanly to another. The Fig-6 transfer failure is therefore positive evidence for readout-relativity, and it predicts a testable consequence -- marginalizing the artifacts out should improve transfer.

NEAR-MISS HONESTY. Each clause of the fix has partial precedent, but no method satisfies their conjunction. ToMe [4] uses pairwise key-similarity, a genuine redundancy signal, but applies a FIXED per-block merge schedule, learns no rate, does no pruning, and performs NO artifact-marginalization. DiffRate [5] unifies prune and merge and makes the rate differentiable, but drives the entire partition off a SINGLE CLS readout -- it is exactly the method that exposes the conflation. DynamicViT [6], EViT [7], A-ViT [8], Evo-ViT [9], and SPViT [10] use learned or attention-based importance for PRUNING ONLY; none adds a separate redundancy axis or marginalizes over readouts. IA-RED2 [15] and ATS [16] reduce redundancy via a single learned or sampling policy, not a two-axis prune/merge split. Token Pooling [18] and ToMe-for-diffusion [19] are merge-only variants with no artifact-aware estimator. ToFu [17] explicitly BRIDGES pruning and merging, but still derives both from one importance/similarity pipeline, with no readout-ensemble marginalization and no differentiable FLOPs-budgeted learned partition with a collapse guarantee. The artifact literature [11][12] DIAGNOSES the contamination but prescribes architectural or serving fixes and never feeds the diagnosis back into a compression operator. The NAS-budget analogues ProxylessNAS [13] and ViT-Slim [14] supply differentiable-budget machinery but over channels, not a per-block token partition.

By elimination, the SIRC fix is the operator that no one has built: replace the single importance scalar with TWO explicit axes -- a prune-importance axis (dispensability) and a redundancy-based merge axis (pairwise similarity) -- MARGINALIZE each axis over a READOUT ENSEMBLE (multiple layers/heads and a light augmentation family) to average out attention-sink/register and position/scale artifacts, and combine them with a LEARNED PER-BLOCK PARTITION that allocates the block budget between prune-share and merge-share, all still under DiffRate's differentiable FLOPs budget. We call this operator DART -- Disentangled-Axis, Readout-marginalized Token compression.

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

In DiffRate-style token compression, does decomposing the single CLS-attention importance readout -- which conflates prune-dispensability, merge-redundancy, and attention-sink/register artifacts -- into a separate prune-importance axis and a redundancy-based merge axis, each marginalized over a readout ensemble and combined by a learned per-block prune/merge partition, improve the FLOPs-versus-Top-1-accuracy trade-off over DiffRate on ImageNet-1k, given that the operator provably recomputes DiffRate's exact keep-set and FLOPs when the ensemble is the single original readout and the partition is frozen to DiffRate's rate?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

Token compression is THE dominant FLOPs lever for deploying ViTs, because attention cost scales as ~O(|V|^2) and MLP cost as ~O(|V|), so fusing or dropping tokens cuts compute almost linearly-to-quadratically with little parameter change [1][2][3]. DiffRate [5] turns this into a near-free deployment win (~30% FLOPs cut at near-zero Top-1 loss on ImageNet-1k) -- but only "when the keep-set is chosen well", and DiffRate's own Fig-1 shows a ~5-point Top-1 swing at matched FLOPs that is attributable entirely to keep-set choice, i.e., to the ranking signal [4][7]. Because any method that ranks tokens by CLS-attention importance inherits the same artifact contamination diagnosed by the registers and attention-sink literature [11][12], a cleaner importance estimator is a contribution to an entire METHOD FAMILY, not one system: it is a better SIGNAL that pruners and mergers alike can adopt, with an exact collapse back to DiffRate as a safety floor. The broader implications are concrete. First, cleaner importance estimation directly lowers efficient-ViT serving and on-device vision cost at a fixed accuracy target. Second, it gives a principled explanation -- and a predicted improvement -- for DiffRate's imperfect cross-architecture rate transfer (Fig-6): if importance is readout-relative, artifact-marginalization should transfer better across backbones, a testable consequence that shapes how future work ports compression schedules between models. Third, splitting the axes yields an interpretable audit of WHICH axis (prune-dispensability vs merge-redundancy) actually drives compression in each block, useful diagnostics for practitioners tuning compression for a deployment target. The claim that a cleaner importance estimator benefits an entire method family is a predicted consequence of the analysis, not a demonstrated result; we scope it as a hypothesis pending cross-method empirical validation.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

The difficulty is real but bounded. (a) THE AXES ARE NOT INDEPENDENTLY OBSERVABLE: the CLS readout DiffRate uses simultaneously reflects genuine task value, attention-sink/register salience [11][12], and position/scale bias, so from one scalar you cannot separate "dispensable" (prune) from "redundant" (merge) -- a token can be important AND redundant, or unimportant AND unique, and a single ordering cannot encode both. (b) THE NAIVE FIX DOES NOT RE-COMPOSE: simply running two separate scorers fails the engineering bar because two independent rankings do not reproduce DiffRate's keep-set, leaving no anchor that proves the change is a clean generalization rather than a different method; worse, prune and merge INTERACT through the shared FLOPs budget, since merging changes the token count and the residual saliency distribution and therefore what is worth pruning, and both draw from the same per-block budget c_b. (c) MONTE-CARLO MARGINALIZATION IS BIASED AT SMALL K AND COSTS FORWARD PASSES: the prune estimate is unbiased, but the merge estimate contains an inner max over partners and is positively biased for finite K, and each extra readout/augmentation is extra forward-pass cost; without variance control (z-normalization, common-random-numbers, a softened top-m partner, warmup at K_max) the dose-response curve is confounded by estimator noise. (d) DIFFERENTIABLE PARTITION UNDER A HARD FLOPs CONSTRAINT: keep-counts are DISCRETE (n_prune and n_merge come from round((1 - pi_b) * c_b)), so making pi_b learnable under a hard budget needs DiffRate's DDP-style soft-mask relaxation, and a naive straight-through estimator breaks the forward/backward consistency that must hold for the forward pass to stay a valid hard partition respecting c_b. (e) CAUSAL ATTRIBUTION, NOT JUST A BETTER SCHEDULE: any added capacity plus re-tuning can nudge accuracy up, making "improvement" unfalsifiable, so the gain must be made auditable. Why it is nonetheless tractable: public checkpoints exist for every model (timm DeiT-T/S/B [2], official MAE ViT-B/L [3]), so the no-fine-tune regime needs only inference; the extra compute is bounded and explicit (K forward passes per image, K small, e.g. <= 8, amortizable to the last few blocks' attention that is already computed, plus one scalar theta_b per block); and the collapse identity guarantees a correct floor at DiffRate, so research risk is bounded to "how much above the floor".

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The novelty wedge is a conjunction that NO existing token-compression method satisfies: DART (a) splits prune-IMPORTANCE from merge-REDUNDANCY as two explicitly different decisions, (b) MARGINALIZES each estimate over a readout ensemble (layers/heads/augmentations) specifically to remove attention-sink/register artifacts, and (c) LEARNS a per-block prune/merge partition under a DIFFERENTIABLE FLOPs budget with an EXACT collapse identity back to DiffRate. Each clause alone has partial precedent -- ToMe [4] has redundancy but no learned rate, no pruning, no marginalization; DiffRate [5] has the differentiable budget but one readout; DynamicViT/EViT/A-ViT/Evo-ViT/SPViT [6][7][8][9][10], IA-RED2 [15], and ATS [16] prune off a single signal; Token Pooling [18] and ToMe-for-diffusion [19] are merge-only; ToFu [17] bridges prune and merge but from one pipeline with no marginalization and no collapse-guaranteed learned partition; ProxylessNAS [13] and ViT-Slim [14] supply differentiable budgets over channels, not tokens -- but their CONJUNCTION, and especially clause (b) feeding an artifact-aware estimate into the compression operator, does not exist. Two structural reasons explain the gap, not oversight. First, the artifact literature POST-DATES most compression methods: DynamicViT/EViT/A-ViT/Evo-ViT/SPViT and even ToMe and DiffRate (2021-2023) were designed before "Vision Transformers Need Registers" [11] and the attention-sink analyses [12] crystallized in late 2023 / ICLR 2024, so methods built before the diagnosis literally could not marginalize out an artifact they had no name for. Second, PRUNE and MERGE evolved as SEPARATE sub-fields with distinct lineages and benchmarks, so the conceptual need to treat dispensability and redundancy as TWO axes only became pressing once DiffRate unified them under one readout and thereby made the conflation visible. DART sits exactly at that new intersection -- it needs both the unified differentiable operator (to have a partition worth learning) and the post-2023 artifact diagnosis (to know what to marginalize out) -- a combination that did not co-exist until now. DART differs from and improves on prior work by being the first to convert the registers/attention-sink finding into a compression estimator, and the exact collapse identity keeps the positioning honest: gains, if any, come from the added axes, not reimplementation drift.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

FORMALIZATION (two axes). For block b with tokens t = 1..N_b (excluding CLS), let A^{(l,h)}_CLS(t) be the CLS-to-token attention at layer l, head h, and let k(t), v(t) be the key/value vectors. The PRUNE-IMPORTANCE axis (dispensability; high = keep) for a single readout is s_prune^{(l,h)}(t) = A^{(l,h)}_CLS(t) -- exactly DiffRate's signal. The MERGE-REDUNDANCY axis (duplication; high = safe to fuse) uses ToMe-style bipartite matching: split tokens into sets X, Y (alternating index partition) and score, in key space, s_merge(t) = max over t' in Y of cos(k(t), k(t')) = <k(t),k(t')> / (||k(t)|| ||k(t')||). These are different objects -- s_prune is per-token saliency, s_merge is a pairwise duplication measure -- so a token can be HIGH s_prune and HIGH s_merge (merge it) or LOW on both (prune it), which DiffRate's single ordering cannot express.

READOUT-ENSEMBLE MARGINALIZATION + MC ESTIMATOR. Define a readout family R = {r_1..r_K}, each r_i a (layer, head) pair drawn from a chosen set L x H (e.g. the last 3 blocks' attention maps and a subset of heads), optionally composed with a light label-preserving augmentation a ~ q(a) (horizontal flip, small +/- scale jitter in {0.9, 1.0, 1.1}, small crop). Artifact causes are readout-relative, so the marginalized estimates s_prune_bar(t) = E_r[s_prune^{(r)}(t; a)] and s_merge_bar(t) = E_r[s_merge^{(r)}(t; a)] suppress them while content-driven signal survives. The Monte-Carlo estimator over K draws is s_prune_hat(t) = (1/K) sum_i s_prune^{(r_i)}(t; a_i) and s_merge_hat(t) = (1/K) sum_i s_merge^{(r_i)}(t; a_i). Per-readout scores are z-normalized before averaging so per-block magnitude drift (cause c) does not let one readout dominate; s_prune_hat is unbiased, while s_merge_hat's inner max is positively biased at finite K, controlled with common-random-numbers (fixed X/Y partition across readouts), a softened top-m partner average for the gradient path, and warmup at K = K_max.

LEARNED PER-BLOCK PARTITION pi_b UNDER DIFFRATE'S BUDGET. For each block learn a logit theta_b and set pi_b = sigmoid(theta_b) in [0,1]. Given DiffRate's DDP-decided per-block compression count c_b (still produced by the differentiable rate r(b) under the global FLOPs constraint -- DART only SPLITS c_b), allocate n_prune(b) = round((1 - pi_b) * c_b) and n_merge(b) = c_b - n_prune(b): prune the n_prune(b) tokens with lowest s_prune_hat, then merge the n_merge(b) remaining tokens with highest s_merge_hat into their partners (ToMe weighted average with proportional attention re-weighting). theta_b and r(b) train jointly under the task loss plus DiffRate's FLOPs-constraint loss; the non-differentiable round() uses DiffRate's DDP-style soft mask with a straight-through estimator on the hard forward selection and the soft expected mask on the backward pass. The only new learnable parameters are {theta_b} (one scalar per block), keeping added capacity tiny and auditable.

COLLAPSE UNIT TEST (B1). At the degenerate config -- readout ensemble size 1 equal to DiffRate's original single CLS readout (same layer/head, identity augmentation), merge axis slaved to the importance order, and partition frozen so (n_prune, n_merge) equals DiffRate's split -- DART must RECOMPUTE DiffRate exactly: assert per-block kept/pruned/merged index-set equality set(keep_dart[b]) == set(keep_diff[b]) for all b, and |FLOPs_new - FLOPs_DiffRate| <= 1e-6. Any divergence at this setting is a bug, not a contribution; this guarantees a correct floor at DiffRate by construction.

DATASETS / MODELS. ImageNet-1k (1.28M train / 50k val, 224x224); DeiT-Tiny/-Small/-Base [2] and MAE ViT-Base/-Large [3] using public checkpoints (timm DeiT, official MAE), in two regimes: (a) NO fine-tuning (plug-in compression on the frozen checkpoint, isolating ranking quality) and (b) 30-epoch fine-tuning matching DiffRate's setup.

METRICS. Primary: GFLOPs vs Top-1 at MATCHED FLOPs budgets equal to DiffRate's operating points (e.g. DeiT-S ~2.9 GFLOPs from ~4.6 dense; MAE ViT-B ~11.5 GFLOPs from ~17.6 dense), over several budgets. Throughput in images/sec on a single fixed GPU at batch 256 (so any K-induced overhead shows). AXIS-ATTRIBUTION: per block report prune-share = n_prune(b)/c_b and merge-share = pi_b, an interpretable learned split DiffRate cannot produce. FAIRNESS/ROBUSTNESS: report per-class and worst-group Top-1 accuracy under compression to audit whether token dropping disproportionately harms small-object or under-represented categories in ImageNet-1k.

BASELINES. DiffRate [5] (primary, same checkpoints/budgets), ToMe [4], EViT [7], DynamicViT [6], A-ViT [8], Evo-ViT [9], SPViT [10], all compared at matched GFLOPs.

FALSIFIABILITY TRIAD. (i) COLLAPSE: B1 must pass (index-set equality + |dFLOPs| <= 1e-6). (ii) DUAL PLACEBO: Leg-1 (structure-destroying) replaces the ensemble with random/shuffled readouts or splits c_b by a random pi_b -- same extra compute, claimed mechanism destroyed -- and must NOT beat DiffRate (if it does, the gain is capacity/tuning, not disentanglement, and the idea is FALSIFIED); Leg-2 (absent-mechanism) is single readout (K=1) + frozen partition and must EQUAL DiffRate (the B1 anchor in experiment form). (iii) DOSE-RESPONSE: vary K = 1, 2, 4, 8, K_max at fixed FLOPs, predicting a MONOTONE-then-PLATEAU Top-1 gain (a flat or non-monotone curve weakens the marginalization claim). Mechanism ablations add prune-axis-only vs merge-axis-only vs both (expect both > either), frozen vs learned partition (expect learned >= frozen), and a re-run of DiffRate's cross-architecture transfer (Fig-6) testing whether marginalization improves transfer.

EXPECTED OUTCOMES. At matched FLOPs, a MODEST but CONSISTENT +0.2 to +0.8 Top-1 over DiffRate; LARGER gains up to ~+1.0 where the mechanism bites hardest -- the no-fine-tuning regime (ranking quality matters more) and models with stronger artifact tokens (larger ViTs / MAE, which exhibit register/sink behavior [11][12]); IMPROVED cross-architecture transfer vs DiffRate's Fig-6 (smaller drop when rates are transferred, because the marginalized axes are less structure-specific); and an INTERPRETABLE per-block prune/merge split (early blocks lean merge on redundant low-level tokens, later blocks lean prune on dispensable tokens), a qualitative result DiffRate cannot report. The idea is FALSIFIED if Leg-1 placebo helps as much as the real method, if B1 fails, or if the dose-response is flat.

## 4. References and Evidence

[1] Dosovitskiy et al. "An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale." ICLR 2021. (ViT)

[2] Touvron et al. "Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention." ICML 2021. (DeiT)

[3] He et al. "Masked Autoencoders Are Scalable Vision Learners." CVPR 2022. (MAE)

[4] Bolya, Fu, Dai, Zhang, Feichtenhofer, Hoffman. "Token Merging: Your ViT But Faster." ICLR 2023. (ToMe)

[5] Chen, Fan, Wang, Lu, et al. "DiffRate: Differentiable Compression Rate for Efficient Vision Transformers." ICCV 2023. (DiffRate -- target method)

[6] Rao, Zhao, Liu, Lu, Zhou, Hsieh. "DynamicViT: Efficient Vision Transformers with Dynamic Token Sparsification." NeurIPS 2021.

[7] Liang, Ge, Tong, Song, Wang, Xie. "Not All Patches are What You Need: Expediting Vision Transformers via Token Reorganizations." ICLR 2022. (EViT)

[8] Yin, Vahdat, Alvarez, Mallya, Kautz, Molchanov. "A-ViT: Adaptive Tokens for Efficient Vision Transformer." CVPR 2022.

[9] Xu, Zhang, Zhang, Tao. "Evo-ViT: Slow-Fast Token Evolution for Dynamic Vision Transformer." AAAI 2022.

[10] Kong, Dong, Ma, et al. "SPViT: Enabling Faster Vision Transformers via Latency-Aware Soft Token Pruning." ECCV 2022.

[11] Darcet, Oquab, Mairal, Bojanowski. "Vision Transformers Need Registers." ICLR 2024.

[12] Xiao, Tian, Chen, Han, Lewis. "Efficient Streaming Language Models with Attention Sinks." ICLR 2024. (StreamingLLM -- attention-sink basis)

[13] Cai, Zhu, Han. "ProxylessNAS: Direct Neural Architecture Search on Target Task and Hardware." ICLR 2019.

[14] Chavan, Shen, Liu, Xing. "Vision Transformer Slimming: Multi-Dimension Searching in Continuous Optimization Space." CVPR 2022. (ViT-Slim)

[15] Pan, Panda, Jiang, Wang, Feris, Oliva. "IA-RED2: Interpretability-Aware Redundancy Reduction for Vision Transformers." NeurIPS 2021.

[16] Fayyaz, Koohpayegani, Jafari, et al. "Adaptive Token Sampling for Efficient Vision Transformers." ECCV 2022. (ATS)

[17] Kim, Gao, Hsu, Shen, Jin. "Token Fusion: Bridging the Gap between Token Pruning and Token Merging." WACV 2024. (ToFu)

[18] Marin, Chang, Ranjan, Prabhu, Rastegari, Tuzel. "Token Pooling in Vision Transformers for Image Classification." WACV 2023 (arXiv, approx 2021).

[19] Bolya, Hoffman. "Token Merging for Fast Stable Diffusion." CVPR Workshops (arXiv, approx 2023). (ToMe-for-diffusion)

[20] Liu, Lin, Cao, Hu, Wei, Zhang, Lin, Guo. "Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows." ICCV 2021.
